skill.name: google-agents-cli-adk-code
skill.description: Reference skill for writing Google Agent Development Kit Python agent code. It covers agent definitions, tools, callbacks, state, memory, multi-agent orchestration, and ADK workflow patterns.
skill.summary: Build Google ADK Python agents using concise reference patterns for tools, state, callbacks, and workflows.
skill.author: google

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many command, network, credential, and code-execution patterns, but review shows they are mainly Markdown reference examples for Google ADK. The skill is not malicious and no prompt injection attempt was found, but it does teach agents to create code that can use external commands, network tools, API credentials, and code execution. Publish with a medium-risk warning for users who enable generated tools or run suggested commands.

medium_findings.title: Reference Patterns for Code Execution
medium_findings.description: The skill documents ADK code executor usage, including model-internal code execution. This is legitimate ADK guidance, but generated agents that enable it should use sandboxing, input limits, and explicit user approval.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines explicitly show ADK code executor configuration. The context is documentation rather than hidden execution, so the risk is capability misuse, not confirmed malicious behavior.

medium_findings.title: Network-Capable Tooling Guidance
medium_findings.description: The skill teaches use of Google Search grounding, web page loading, remote MCP servers, and remote A2A agents. These are expected ADK features, but they can send requests to external services when implemented by generated agent code.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The documentation clearly covers network-enabled ADK integrations. No evidence shows covert exfiltration, but the capability requires user review before use.

medium_findings.title: Credential and Environment Configuration Examples
medium_findings.description: The references mention environment files, API key variables, Vertex AI variables, and authentication helpers. This is normal setup guidance, but users should avoid exposing secrets in prompts, generated code, logs, or shared repositories.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines explicitly reference .env files, API key variables, and auth helpers. The material does not request secrets or transmit them, so this is a controlled configuration risk.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backticks Misclassified as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: Many static external command findings are false positives caused by inline Markdown code spans and fenced Python examples. The skill files are reference documents and do not contain executable Ruby or shell backtick logic.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The matched tokens are Markdown formatting around API names and references. No runnable shell interpolation or Ruby backtick execution is present in these locations.

low_findings.title: Weak Crypto Findings Are Terminology False Positives
low_findings.description: The high weak-cryptography findings map to words such as task, hash, and route in ADK documentation. No MD5, SHA-1, DES, or custom cryptographic implementation was found in the reviewed context.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Reviewed lines describe ADK modes, event fields, and skill metadata, not cryptographic algorithms. The finding appears caused by broad keyword matching.

dangerous_patterns.title: Code Executor Enablement
dangerous_patterns.description: Documents BuiltInCodeExecutor and Vertex AI Code Executor patterns that can execute model-generated code when used in an agent.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is explicit and capability-related, but it is documented as an ADK feature rather than hidden skill behavior.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Tool and Server Connections
dangerous_patterns.description: Documents web loading, MCP stdio/SSE connections, remote A2A agents, and official documentation fetching.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The examples clearly enable network or external process connectivity. The context is transparent documentation, not covert outbound traffic.

dangerous_patterns.title: Credential Handling in Generated Agent Code
dangerous_patterns.description: Documents API key, service account, and OAuth helper patterns that require careful secret handling in generated implementations.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The credential-related guidance is explicit. It is normal for ADK setup, but mishandling secrets would create downstream risk.

content.value_statement: Developers need reliable patterns when writing Google ADK agents, tools, callbacks, and workflows. This skill provides concise Python references that help Claude, Codex, and Claude Code generate ADK code faster.
actual_capabilities: Provides Python examples for defining ADK agents, LlmAgent instances, BaseAgent classes, and workflow agents.
Shows patterns for function tools, ToolContext usage, confirmations, authentication, OpenAPI tools, MCP tools, and built-in tools.
Explains state, memory, artifacts, sessions, callbacks, plugins, and context caching in ADK projects.
Covers multi-agent orchestration with SequentialAgent, ParallelAgent, LoopAgent, AgentTool, delegation, and task mode.
Documents graph workflow concepts including nodes, edges, routes, fan-out, fan-in, HITL, retries, and dynamic node scheduling.
Points users toward related Google Agents CLI skills for workflow, scaffolding, evaluation, and deployment tasks.
limitations: The reference is Python-focused and does not provide full coverage for other ADK language SDKs.
It does not scaffold new projects or deploy agents; related skills handle those workflows.
Examples are cheatsheet patterns and may need verification against the installed ADK version.
Network, credential, and code-execution examples require security review before production use.

use_cases.title: Create an ADK Agent Prototype
use_cases.description: Use the skill to draft a Python root agent with instructions, tools, state keys, and model settings.

use_cases.title: Design Multi-Agent Orchestration
use_cases.description: Use the skill to compare sequential, parallel, loop, delegation, and task-mode patterns for a complex workflow.

use_cases.title: Add Production Agent Capabilities
use_cases.description: Use the skill to add callbacks, memory, artifacts, confirmations, auth helpers, and workflow graph controls.

prompt_templates.title: Draft a Basic Agent
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the Google ADK code skill to create a simple Python agent for [task]. Include the root_agent definition, instructions, and one typed function tool.

prompt_templates.title: Add Tools and State
prompt_templates.prompt: Using ADK Python patterns, add tools, ToolContext state handling, and confirmation for [sensitive action]. Explain where secrets should be configured.

prompt_templates.title: Plan Multi-Agent Flow
prompt_templates.prompt: Design an ADK multi-agent architecture for [workflow]. Compare SequentialAgent, ParallelAgent, LoopAgent, AgentTool, and task mode before writing code.

prompt_templates.title: Build a Workflow Graph
prompt_templates.prompt: Use ADK Workflow API patterns to implement [graph process]. Include nodes, routes, fan-out or fan-in, retry behavior, HITL handling, and output schema guidance.

output_examples.input: Create a weather assistant using Google ADK.
output_examples.output: A concise implementation plan with a root agent, a typed weather tool, clear instructions, state usage notes, and testing suggestions.

output_examples.input: Design a support triage workflow with human approval.
output_examples.output: A workflow outline with classifier, priority routes, approval pauses, escalation behavior, and notes about resumability and audit trails.

output_examples.input: Add authenticated API access to an ADK agent.
output_examples.output: A security-aware design that separates credentials from prompts, uses ADK auth helpers, and requires confirmation for sensitive API actions.

best_practices: Start from the scaffolded project structure before adding agent code.
Use typed tools, schemas, and explicit state keys so generated agents are easier to test.
Review network, credential, file, and code-execution capabilities before running generated agents.
anti_patterns: Do not paste API keys, OAuth secrets, or service account data into prompts or generated files.
Do not enable code execution, filesystem MCP servers, or remote tools without clear user approval.
Do not use this skill for deployment or project scaffolding when the dedicated Google Agents CLI skills are available.

faq.question: What does this skill help build?
faq.answer: It helps build Python agents with Google ADK, including tools, callbacks, state, memory, and workflow orchestration.

faq.question: Does this skill create a new ADK project?
faq.answer: No. Use the Google Agents CLI scaffold skill for new projects or project enhancement.

faq.question: Can it help with graph workflows?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes ADK Workflow API guidance for nodes, edges, routes, fan-out, fan-in, HITL, and retries.

faq.question: Is it safe to use with credentials?
faq.answer: Use caution. The skill explains auth patterns, but credentials should stay in secure environment configuration.

faq.question: Does it support Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The report marks support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Why is the security risk medium?
faq.answer: The files are documentation, but they teach patterns involving network access, external commands, credentials, and code execution.
